further_info_edited:
On Friday evening 17th June we were sailing in approx 17knots wind,  Speed over ground approx 7knots
at 23:16 hrs the wind died so motor on. (I can give you our position if necessary)

at 05:50hrs on 18th June (Yacht X) a USA sailing boat called us on VHF ch16 they were approx 1.5NM ahead of us.
Skipper of Yacht X reported that they had just had a 15minutes interaction with Orcas. Their Wind Vane steering had been broken off from the stern of the boat, and their rudder was damaged. We discussed what to do and the Skipper of Yacht X whilst "shook-up" said they were okay and did not require assistance. So we decided to alter course to starboard to stay clear of their position and make haste to clear the area.
Some time later, (approx 30minutes later) the Skipper of Yacht X advised that the ORCAs were back near his boat, but as far as I am aware they just swam around and did not do any further damage.
After the earlier VHF call between my yacht and Yacht X,  Yacht Y, a USA sailing boat who had been sailing on the same passage as my yacht all the way from Cascais Marina (Portugal) altered course and headed towards Yacht X.  I don't know why they did that. As far as I am aware there was no communication on VHF channel 16 between Yacht X and Yacht Y.
on 18th June at 06:40hrs UTC Yacht Y who where close to Yacht X issued a PANPAN saying that they had been attacked by a small pod of ORCAS.
They spoke to Tarifa Radio on CH83 to report the attack, but said they did not require assistance.
Tarifa radio provided them with clear instructions regarding what to do if they were a further interaction with ORCAS,
Subsequently Yacht X headed North to CADIZ, and as far as I am aware they did not require assistance, and have reached CADIZ safely and Yacht Y proceeded on to Gibraltar arriving approx 1 hour after my yacht so presumably they were okay as well.

My yacht was motorsailing with Mainsail up during the time of this incident.
at the time of the initial Orca Interation with Yacht X at 0550hrs UTC my yacht was just north of Banco Del Hoyo in 27m water depth at position 36d 08.619N 006d 25.630W

About 1/2 mile out from the entrance of the bay we diverted to stay well clear of sightseeing boat, with  2 unidentified fins swimming close by, these surfaced again inshore of us. further_info_edited: There was thick fog for much of the journey - about 300m visibility much of the time. The reason we gave up following close to the shore, in and out of bays, was that we were twice contacted by fishermen to warn us we were too close to rocks and heading into a bay. On the second occurrence Finisterre Radio joined in the warnings, so we gave up being so cautious and hopped from cape to cape, only keeping close in shore on long sweeping bays. For the 2nd half of the trip we were motoring parallel with about 6 other yachts, but we were closer to the shore, all heading for A Coruña area.
precautions_taken_edited: Kept very close to the coast, especially at first. Mainly less than 20m depth, 500m from shore at first, later we cut across the mouths of significant bays but otherwise continued to keep close to shore in <25m

Local - 38º13,83'N . 9º5,67'W 
Between Sines and Cabo Espichel when going north, I saw a Orca going south at 300m West. 
Immediately, I turn off the engine, auto-pilot and all eletronics devices and left the boat adrift (without touching the rudder).

We hugged the coastline very closely wherever possible, staying in less than 10m depth unless forced further out by tuna farm nets.  We draw 2.4m.   There are a few tuna nets but not as many as we had expected from reports of the area being littered with them, and those that we saw were all marked on the chart within cardinal buoyed areas.
There is a large net/farm that comes out from the entrance to Barbate but we were able to sail between the northern end and the entrance to Barbate through a narrow passage approx 100m wide.  We were then forced offshore twice to go around buoyed marine farms between Barbate and Tarifa.

further_info_edited:
1. We counted about 14 other boats doing a similar passage during the day. 
2. When rounding the tunny net W of Tarifa, the French catamaran 1/2 mile ahead kindly called us on the VHF to say he had seen orcas. We both motored fast towards shallow water & found passage inside tunny nets to round Tarifa. We did not see the orcas. 
3. The marina office in La Linea told us that there had been an orca attack today near Tarifa & the victim had made a reservation to be lifted out. Obviously , this is unconfirmed .
precautions_taken_edited:
1. We remained within 1/2- 1 mile of the coast nearly all the time, mostly in 5-15m depth. 
2. When we were forced out by tunny nets, we motored within 50m of the nets, following them closely out & then closely back in. 
3. Where possible, we went inside the tunny nets such as at Barbate & at Tarifa. We learned subsequently that it was possible to go inshore of the tunny nets close to Zahara. 
4. We motored all the time to retain maximum flexibility in the event of attack. 
5. We chose a still day with slight sea & wind below 10 knots. 
6. We monitored closely all the available information sources, especially for where recent attacks had taken place. 
7. Although we used the auto-pilot, we always had someone close to enable coming out instantly.
8. We maintained a continuous stern watch in the peak danger areas. 
9. We prepared for an attack - by wearing lifejackets , had two full buckets of sand and various means of making noise. 
10. We kept the VHF on Ch 16 all the time. 
11. We had pingers but  not sure of their value.

The passage was to sail the yacht from La Linea de la Conception, Spain to Faro, Portugal. 
The precautions taken were:-
Monitor Orca Incident reports on Orcinus and the traffic lights to gain a sense of the level of threat and where the threat may be.
Break the passage into day sails instead of longer and overnight sails. La Linea - Barbate - Cadiz - Mazagon - Ayamonte - Faro.

further_info_edited: Another yacht reported an attack to Tarifa traffic.  We had passed them earlier in the day . Their AIS showed them to be about 10 miles astern of us and quite a lot further inshore in fact in less than 20 m depths it seemed.
precautions_taken_edited:
We were on passage from Horta to La Linea (Alcaidesa) .  We had no mainsail and only limited fuel so wanted to keep the distance as short as practical.  

Our yacht had been away from Europe since the autumn.  The original plan was to leave her in the Azores for the summer and so I had not followed the Orca situation closely.  When it became clear we were not going to get a repair in Horta anytime soon and decided to keep going , I looked at the CA's data and Orcinus and read a little on the web.  The thrust of which was that most of the incidents had been south and east of  Cadiz. (There were a couple of sightings inshore around Faro , I recall).  The boats affected were largely less than 50ft.  There was a thread running about the colour of antifoul - black seemed to be a contributor.   We are all but 60ft LOA and have blue antifouling.   At that stage I hadn’t considered the Ultrasonic antifoul point.

We rounded close to Cape St Vincent and sailed directly towards Cadiz. About 15 miles off we came into soundings  and headed approx. SE to clear the Trafalgar Bank. Once we had cleared it, the wind was W10-15kts - we didn’t have the fuel to motor and we would have made little progress dead downwind under headsail alone so we sailed with the wind on our quarter aiming inside the shallows off Punta Paloma de los Corrales and thence to Tarifa. Between the Trafalgar Bank and Paloma de los Corrales we were in 50-100m of water.   

It was on that stretch when another yacht was attacked, close inshore around Punta de Gracia/Camarinal - at the time their AIS signal put them in less than 20m.

Now we are in Europe and I have access to the news etc, that was perhaps not our best option - but that is what we did. 

The thought about Ultrasonic antifoul arose as a consideration subsequently.   It is a fact that if you have a diver under the boat they will quickly ask you to turn it off - the noise is hard to tolerate - but you have to quite close to the boat to hear it.   Whales may have more sensitive hearing but they travel quite quickly and I would have thought there would be loads of other noise around them to distract them.

further_info_edited: We passed Barbate heading for the Straits of Gibraltar at 5pm on May 4th, north of the TSS.  When we arrived at Motril we heard of the story of a Beneteau 50' (i think) coming from Madeira, at exactly a similar position to us but on the southern side of the TSS and only 10 hours later, that was hit by Orca's, and as the rescue vessel was towing the boat into Barbate it sank near the entrance.  Everyone, thankfully, was OK, even though Orca's do not attack humans, but they survived the ordeal.  Close shave, i think it's called.
precautions_taken_edited: Kept in shallow water, but cut the corner of the Gulf of Cadiz slightly.

further_info_edited:
After leaving A Coruna we headed about 3 miles off the coast to get some wind. We observed other sailing yachts going closer to shore under engine, which seems to be the strategy of the majority in light conditions. IN retrospect we would have gone a bit closer to Finisterre, something that got confirmed later by a yacht being attacked 24 miles in our wake. The attack happened around midnight and the next day another boat got attacked nearby the previous. That would suggest these pods usually hang around for at least a day or two, which again would suggest it's important to follow the reports and NOT enter an area with recent attacks.

When getting past Vigo we got plenty of wind and the boat was doing around 10 knots. We shifted lookout focus to the forward sector and luckily we had plenty of lunar light.

The rest of the trip was uneventful keeping ever so near the coast. As a curiosity we met another boat run by a very experienced skipper that gave us the advice to stay at depths more than a 1000 meters. He did not specify it further.
precautions_taken_edited: Staying close to shore and monitoring VHF on channel 11 and 16 + keeping a good eyeball lookout.  One attack occurred on a boat 24 miles behind us close to Finisterre. Boat did not need assistance afterwards.

Next twelve hours from approx abreast sines to above Lisbon in darkness and motoring only approx 6 knots - no wind

Final 6 hours above Lisbon to nazare in daylight motoring close to shipping lane then  direct route to nazare.

Other than that we kept to the shipping lanes because we wanted to sail at night and not get be caught in all the fishing buoys that are close in to the coast. On the last day we motored because there was a hurricane crossing towards the Portuguese coast.

further_info_edited: Our passage included a straight line from La Rochelle (France) to Castro Urdiales (Spain), including shallow (<20m) and very deep (>1000m) waters. Conditions varied from 3-5Bf winds and 2-3m swell at the start, with clouded skies and some showers; to decreasing to 1-3Bf winds and <1m swell towards the end, with clear sky.
precautions_taken_edited:
We prepared an orca risk assessment plan for our travels in 2025, which include sailing from Arzal (France) to the Med (April-June), and from the Med to the Canary Islands onto Cape Verde (Sep-Nov).

We checked orca presence before and at time of departure using the CA table, the GTOA website, and the orca.pt website and Telegram groups. Based on the information from these groups, no orcas had been sighted in the Bay of Biscaye in April 2025 (and 2024 and 2023), bar from one interaction listed on the GTAO website in April 2025.

We listed our travel plan on the orca.pt Telegram group, and took no further precautions.

Wired up additional bilge pump for use in bulkhead area near rudder, to pump out any water ingress if it occurred
Normal preparations for the possibility of 'abandon ship' eg lifejackets on, grab bag ready etc 
Did not release holding tanks when in water of depth where orcas are found - no idea if additional nutrients in the water could attract them!
Logged voyage with orcas.pt before departure
